visual_goal: A clean teaching transformation demo where one continuous hero mesh visibly morphs through compatible target silhouettes using sparse vertex markers, thin trajectory traces, and compact formula/t controls.
critic_priorities: keep exactly one hero mesh on screen at all times
make per-vertex correspondence and straight-line interpolation visible
keep formulas and t controls readable without covering the mesh
preserve shared topology across cube, sphere, capsule, and rounded star
maintain a clean soft-floor teaching aesthetic

must_have: one centered hero mesh morphing continuously
about 20 sparse vertex dots visible on the surface
thin trajectory lines during cube-to-sphere interpolation
readable formula overlay and t slider in a side panel
cube, sphere, capsule, and rounded star share topology
must_avoid: dots on every vertex making the mesh noisy
axes, grid arrows, or generic coordinate-frame clutter
formula overlay covering the hero mesh
aesthetic_goals: soft floor with neutral gradient background
liquid-metal smooth motion with crisp cube holds
subtle lighting from two soft key lights and one rim light
blocking_conditions: more than one hero mesh appears
morphing is replaced by cuts between separate objects
target shapes do not appear to share the same vertex set
required formulas or t progression are absent

repair_policy: remove duplicate or side-by-side target meshes and keep only the morphing hero mesh
replace axes or grid helpers with sparse vertex dots and thin interpolation traces
move formulas and t slider into a compact side panel away from the mesh center
reduce vertex markers to a readable sparse subset if the surface becomes cluttered
replace any topology-changing target with a same-index deformation of the shared mesh
restore soft floor, neutral background, and subtle studio lighting if the scene becomes dark or sci-fi
forbidden_abstractions: cube sphere capsule and star shown as separate side-by-side objects
shape morphing represented as a hard cut instead of continuous vertex motion
target shapes generated with different vertex counts or inconsistent indexing
rounded star replaced by a torus or any hole-bearing topology
all vertices covered with markers until the surface becomes unreadable
generic transformation classroom axes overpowering the morphing mesh
black void or foggy sci-fi stage replacing the soft teaching floor
